codeReview.summary: Clean implementation covering all three requirements: (1) lang-names in mt.clj updated to contain exactly 20 languages (10 tier-1 + 10 tier-2) by replacing 7 non-tier languages with the 7 missing tier-2 codes. (2) Five bilingual word tables added to codemix.clj for Latin-script tier-2 languages (tr, vi, id, sw, tl), each with 20+ word pairs — well exceeding the >=5 minimum. (3) 17 new tests with 101 assertions covering lang-names completeness, word-table presence, code-mix output validity, seed determinism, and cross-language differentiation. All new tests pass; full suite shows only 4 pre-existing proxy-related errors. Translations are linguistically reasonable. Minor observations below.

issues.description: lang-names changed from ^:private to public to allow test access. This widens the API surface. An alternative would be to use (var mt/lang-names) in tests or add a public query function. However, since lang-names is pure data that other namespaces (e.g., pipeline config, coverage metrics) may legitimately need, making it public is defensible.

word-tables changed from ^:private to public for the same reason. Same trade-off applies. The codemix code itself only uses word-tables internally via (get word-tables l2 {}), so the encapsulation change is solely for testability.

VAL-MULTI-004 specifies 'MT transform produces valid (non-empty, different from input) translations for at least 3 tier-2 languages' but the tests only verify lang-names completeness, not actual MT proxy output. This is understandable since the proxy requires PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN which isn't available in the test environment (evidenced by the 4 pre-existing proxy test errors). The lang-names coverage is the code-level prerequisite; MT output validation would need an integration test with the live proxy.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker changed two vars from ^:private to public (mt/lang-names and codemix/word-tables) to enable direct test access. The project's AGENTS.md doesn't document a convention on when to use ^:private vs public for data vars, or how tests should access private vars (e.g., via #'ns/var or public query functions). This could lead to inconsistency across features.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: mt.clj:29 — `(def lang-names ...)` was `(def ^:private lang-names ...)`; codemix.clj:22 — `(def word-tables ...)` was `(def ^:private word-tables ...)`

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker acknowledged skipping the RED-GREEN TDD cycle in skillFeedback, noting it's less useful for data-addition features. The skill procedure says 'Write tests first (RED)' without exception. The deviation is justified — testing pure data maps before they exist provides no diagnostic value — but the skill could note this exception explicitly.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'The skill says Write tests first (RED) but for a data-addition feature like this (adding language entries to maps), writing tests before the data exists would just mean running them to see failures with no code logic to debug.'

summary: PASS. All three feature requirements met: lang-names has all 20 languages, 5 tier-2 word tables added with 20+ pairs each, and 17 tests with 101 assertions verify completeness and functionality. No blocking issues. Non-blocking notes: (1) two private vars made public for testability, (2) MT proxy output not tested for tier-2 (requires live proxy). Code is clean, idiomatic Clojure, linguistically reasonable translations, good test coverage including determinism and cross-language differentiation.
